race.name: Taiwan KOM Challenge
race.display_name: Taiwan KOM Challenge — The Ascent to the Clouds
race.tagline: 105 kilometers from the Pacific Ocean to 3,275 meters. Through Taroko Gorge's marble cathedrals to the roof of Taiwan. Asia's most iconic hill climb — where WorldTour pros and amateur dreamers start together at sea level and finish above the clouds.

vitals.date: Late October annually (typically fourth Friday of October)
terrain_types: Flat coastal road from Qixingtan Beach to Taroko Gorge entrance
Narrow gorge road through marble canyons with tunnels and bridges
Sustained mountain climbing through forested valleys at 5-7%
Alpine switchbacks at 10-22% gradient in the final 10 km to Wuling Pass at 3,275m
vitals.start_time: 6:00 AM from Qixingtan Beach Square. First 18.5 km is neutral rollout. Flying start for official timing near Taroko Archway (~6:38 AM).
vitals.registration: Online via taiwankom.org and cyclist.org.tw. Registration opens mid-year, typically closes late September. Open to riders aged 16+. Elite category available for pro teams and riders targeting sub-4-hour finishes.
vitals.prize_purse: Prize money for elite men and women categories. Prize parity announced in 2017 — equal purse for men's and women's elite winners.
vitals.aid_stations: Support stations along the route with water, food, and mechanical assistance. Medical support at key checkpoints. SAG vehicles on course.
vitals.cutoff_time: 6.5 hours for the timed 87 km section (from Taroko Archway to Wuling Pass summit)
distance_options: Taiwan KOM Challenge: 105 km (65 mi) from Qixingtan Beach to Wuling Pass summit at 3,275m — the classic route
2025 alternate: 203 km Classic Euro-style road race (used when traditional Taroko route is unavailable)
vitals.entry_fee: NT$5,000 (~$150 USD) plus NT$500 refundable timing chip deposit

climate.primary: Tropical coast to alpine summit — 25-degree temperature swing in a single ride
climate.description: Late October in Hualien starts warm and humid at sea level (20-25C / 68-77F) with the Pacific coast's tropical influence. As riders climb through Taroko Gorge, temperatures drop steadily. By the time you reach Dayuling at 2,565m, conditions are cool and often misty (5-10C / 41-50F). The final push to Wuling Pass at 3,275m brings near-freezing temperatures (0-5C / 32-41F), potential wind chill, and dramatically thinner air. Riders experience a full seasonal transition in a single effort — from summer coast to winter summit. October sits at the tail end of typhoon season; the 2024 edition was canceled mid-race due to landslides and extreme weather. Afternoon clouds and fog commonly envelope the upper mountain.
challenges: Temperature drop of 20-25C from coastal start to alpine summit — you start in summer and finish in winter
Typhoon season proximity: late October storms can bring landslides, rockfall, and race cancellation (2024 edition canceled mid-race)
Fog and cloud cover on the upper mountain reduce visibility on narrow roads with steep drops
High-altitude cold at 3,275m — wind chill can drive effective temperatures well below freezing
Humidity and warmth in the first 20 km can cause early dehydration that compounds at altitude

terrain.primary: Paved mountain road — sea level to 3,275m through Taroko Gorge
terrain.surface: Provincial Highway 8 (Central Cross-Island Highway) through Taroko National Park. Well-maintained paved road, though the gorge sections feature narrow roads, tunnels carved through marble cliffs, and bridges spanning the Liwu River. The surface ranges from smooth tarmac on the coastal section to rougher mountain road at higher elevations. Post-earthquake road repairs may affect surface quality in some sections.
features: 18.5 km neutral coastal rollout from Qixingtan Beach to Taroko Archway — flat, fast warmup along the Pacific
Taroko Gorge section: narrow road carved through sheer marble cliffs hundreds of meters high, with hand-hewn tunnels and bridges over the Liwu River
Mid-section from Tianxiang to Dayuling: steady 5-7% gradient through forested valleys and open meadows
Final 10 km from Dayuling to Wuling Pass: savage switchbacks averaging 17% with maximum gradients reaching 27%
Altitude gain: sea level to 3,275m (10,745 ft) in a single continuous ascent — one of the highest paved road climbs on Earth
Multiple tunnels through the gorge — some unlit, requiring attention in a mass-start field

gravel_god_rating.tier_note: Asia's premier hill climb — 105 km from the Pacific Ocean to 3,275m through one of the world's great gorges. T1 by score (83). Prestige=4 cannot promote into T1 but score already qualifies. WorldTour pros (Nibali, Yates, Vos, Evans, Pooley) have lined up alongside amateurs at sea level. Course record 3:16:09 (Dyball, 2023).
gravel_god_rating.score_note: elevation=5 for a single continuous climb from sea level to 3,275m — one of the highest paved ascents on Earth. altitude=5 for finishing above 3,275m where oxygen is ~70% of sea level. adventure=5 for Taroko Gorge's marble cathedrals, tunnels, and the transformation from tropical coast to alpine summit. experience=5 for the singular journey of riding from the Pacific Ocean into the clouds on the roof of Taiwan. field_depth=4 for attracting WorldTour Grand Tour contenders (Nibali, Yates, Evans) alongside strong Asian pro field. prestige=4 as Asia's most prestigious cycling event with growing global recognition. race_quality=4 for elite/amateur mass start with chip timing, prize parity, and 40+ nationalities. value=4 for ~$150 entry delivering a world-class mountain course with pro-level organization. cultural_impact=4 as Taiwan's signature cycling event and tourism showcase. Sum of 14=54, cultural_impact=4, total=58, score=round(58/70*100)=83.

logistics.explanation: Getting to Hualien requires genuine effort. International flights land at Taiwan Taoyuan (TPE) or Taipei Songshan (TSA), followed by a 2-3 hour train ride down the east coast to Hualien. There are small domestic flights to Hualien Airport, but most riders take the scenic rail route. Hualien is a mid-sized Taiwanese city with decent hotels at reasonable prices — no shortage of accommodation within 10 km of the start. The event organization is excellent — the Taiwan Cyclist Federation runs a tight operation, and years of experience show. But the fundamental challenge is getting to eastern Taiwan: it is remote by any standard, and for Western riders, the 12-20 hour flights, jet lag, and time zone difference add a layer of logistical complexity that European hill climbs simply do not have.

length.explanation: 105 kilometers of total distance, 87 km of which is timed climbing from the Taroko Archway to Wuling Pass. This is not an ultra-distance event by gran fondo standards, but virtually every kilometer after the neutral section goes uphill. The sustained nature of the effort — 3+ hours of continuous climbing for the elite, 5-6.5 hours for strong amateurs — makes the 105 km distance profoundly different from a flat century. You do not get recovery sections. You do not get descents to spin out your legs. The road goes up, and then it goes up more steeply. The 6.5-hour cutoff for the timed section is generous for strong cyclists but genuinely tight for anyone averaging below 14 km/h on the climb.

technicality.explanation: The road is paved throughout, and the climbing is straightforward in terms of navigation — one road, one direction: up. But the gorge section introduces elements that a typical road climb does not. The tunnels through Taroko Gorge — some hand-carved, some unlit — require attention in a mass-start field of 800+ riders. The road through the gorge is narrow in places, with sheer marble walls on one side and a vertical drop to the Liwu River on the other. There are no technical bike handling demands, but the environment is more demanding than a typical European Alpine switchback. The final 10 km with gradients reaching 27% also require attention to gear selection and pacing — overshoot your effort at 3,000m altitude and there is no recovery.

elevation.explanation: This is one of the great single-ascent climbs on Earth. From sea level to 3,275 meters — 10,745 feet of elevation gain — in a single, unbroken effort. To put this in perspective: the Alpe d'Huez climb is 1,071m. The Stelvio from Prato is 1,808m. The Taiwan KOM more than triples Alpe d'Huez in a single push. The total climbing of approximately 3,500m over 105 km includes the gentle coastal section, but the relentless nature of the ascent — particularly the final 10 km where gradients average 17% and spike to 27% — makes this one of the most punishing elevation profiles in competitive cycling anywhere in the world.

climate.explanation: The climate is not inherently hostile — late October is generally a reasonable time to ride in Taiwan. The challenge is the sheer range of conditions within a single ride. You start in tropical humidity at sea level and finish in near-freezing alpine conditions at 3,275m. The general rule applies: 6 degrees Celsius cooler per 1,000m of ascent. A 22C start means approximately 2C at the summit, before wind chill. October is the tail end of typhoon season — the 2024 edition was canceled mid-race due to landslides and rockfall after only 6 km, and the traditional route is currently closed due to the April 2024 Hualien earthquake. When the weather cooperates, the climate is manageable. When it does not, the event simply cannot happen.

altitude.explanation: Wuling Pass at 3,275 meters is genuine high altitude — the summit sits at an elevation where oxygen availability is approximately 70% of sea level. This is not theoretical. Riders who have never spent time above 2,500m will feel it. Power output drops measurably above 2,000m, and the final 10 km — where gradients are at their steepest — coincide with the altitude where the physiological effects are most pronounced. Altitude sickness is a real concern for riders who push too hard in the final hour. The organizers advise descending immediately if symptoms appear. No amount of fitness compensates for the thin air above 3,000m if you have not acclimatized. For riders coming from sea-level cities — which is most of the international field — this is the defining challenge of the event.

adventure.explanation: There is nowhere else on Earth where you ride from a tropical Pacific beach through marble canyons carved by ancient rivers, past temples perched on cliff edges, through hand-hewn tunnels, across bridges spanning roaring mountain torrents, up through cloud forests, and emerge above the clouds at 3,275 meters — all in a single ride. Taroko Gorge is one of the natural wonders of Asia. The sheer marble walls rise hundreds of meters on either side of the road. The Liwu River cuts through the canyon floor far below. Red bridges span gorges. Buddhist shrines appear in the rock face. The landscape transforms completely — tropical coast, subtropical forest, temperate meadow, alpine scrub, cloud line. By the time you reach Wuling Pass, you have traveled through every climate zone Taiwan possesses. The adventure score is not debatable.

prestige.explanation: The Taiwan KOM is the most prestigious cycling event in Asia. WorldTour Grand Tour contenders — Vincenzo Nibali, Simon Yates, Cadel Evans — have ridden it. Olympic and world champions like Marianne Vos and Emma Pooley have competed in the women's field. The event's position in late October, just after the European racing season ends, makes it a destination for professional riders looking for one more challenge before the off-season. Benjamin Dyball's course record of 3:16:09 is a serious benchmark. John Ebsen's five victories spanning a decade demonstrate the event's longevity and competitive depth. What keeps the prestige at 4 rather than 5 is the event's regional positioning — it is not yet a UCI-sanctioned race, and its global recognition, while growing, does not match the historic European monuments. In Asia, however, this is the pinnacle.

race_quality.explanation: The Taiwan Cyclist Federation organizes a polished event that punches above its weight. The mass start at Qixingtan Beach — 800-1,000 riders launching together at dawn — is dramatic. The neutral rollout through the coast allows the field to spread before the timed section begins. Chip timing on the 87 km timed climb provides accurate competitive results. The elite category attracts professional teams with real tactical racing. Prize parity between men's and women's elite categories was implemented in 2017. Medical support, SAG vehicles, and aid stations are positioned throughout the course. The 6.5-hour cutoff is enforced. The organization handles the complexity of a high-mountain mass-start race in a national park with competence and experience — they have been doing this since 2012. The one deduction: the event's vulnerability to weather cancellation (2024) and route disruption (2025 earthquake damage) is a structural challenge beyond the organizers' control.

experience.explanation: You stand on a beach on the Pacific Ocean at 6:00 AM. The air is warm and humid. The mountain is not visible — it is too far away, too high, hidden behind layers of foothills and cloud. Three to six hours later, depending on who you are, you are standing at 3,275 meters above that ocean. The air is thin and cold. You can see the curvature of the island below you. You rode there. In between, you passed through Taroko Gorge — one of the most spectacular natural formations on Earth — and climbed through every climate zone this island possesses. This is one of cycling's truly transcendent experiences. The transformation from sea level to the roof of Taiwan, through marble canyons and cloud forests, is unlike any other cycling event. It is simultaneously a race, an adventure, and a pilgrimage. Very few rides on Earth deliver this kind of journey in a single day.

community.explanation: The Taiwan KOM draws riders from 40+ countries, creating a genuinely international field that ranges from WorldTour professionals to bucket-list amateurs. The shared pre-race experience in Hualien — a charming coastal city with excellent food — builds some camaraderie. Taiwan's legendary hospitality means international riders are warmly welcomed. However, the community is fundamentally transient: most international riders come for the single event and do not return annually. The Taiwanese domestic cycling community has deeper roots in the event, with local clubs and teams competing every year. Tour operator packages (Alpine Cadence, Taiwan KOM Tours) create small group communities, but the event lacks the years-deep, come-back-every-year tribal identity of events like Unbound or L'Eroica. The community is broad and welcoming, but shallow.

field_depth.explanation: This is where the Taiwan KOM separates itself from other Asian cycling events. The late-October timing — just after the European road season ends — attracts WorldTour riders looking for a final challenge. Vincenzo Nibali won in 2017 and held the course record until 2023. Simon Yates raced in 2023 (11th after a crash). Cadel Evans competed in 2017. Marianne Vos and Ashleigh Moolman-Pasio have contested the women's race. The elite field also includes strong Japanese, Taiwanese, and Southeast Asian continental professionals. Benjamin Dyball's course record of 3:16:09 represents a world-class climbing performance. John Ebsen's five victories demonstrate sustained competitive excellence. The depth is real — finishing in the top 20 means beating legitimate professional cyclists. The amateur field adds volume, but the sharp end of this race is genuinely elite.

value.explanation: NT$5,000 — approximately $150 USD — for entry into one of the world's great hill climbs. For that price you get a mass-start race alongside WorldTour professionals, chip timing on an 87 km timed climb, aid stations, medical support, and the privilege of riding through Taroko National Park on closed roads. By any measure of cost-per-epic-experience, this is outstanding value. The entry fee is a fraction of what comparable European sportives charge. What you are buying is access to a course that would be on any serious cyclist's bucket list even without a race: Taroko Gorge to Wuling Pass is one of the world's iconic road cycling ascents. The race just happens to provide the organizational infrastructure to do it safely and competitively.

expenses.explanation: The entry fee is cheap. Everything else requires a budget. International flights to Taiwan run $800-1,500 from North America or Europe, $200-500 from elsewhere in Asia. The train from Taipei to Hualien is inexpensive (~$25 USD). Hualien hotels are remarkably affordable by international standards — $50-120/night for comfortable accommodation. Food in Taiwan is cheap and extraordinary. The total trip cost for a Western rider: $1,500-2,500 including flights, 3-4 nights accommodation, entry, and meals. For Asian-based riders: $500-1,000. Tour operator packages (Alpine Cadence, Taiwan KOM Tours) run $2,000-2,500 and include everything. Taiwan itself is excellent value once you arrive — it is the getting there that adds up.

biased_opinion.summary: The Taiwan KOM Challenge is one of the most extraordinary single-day efforts in competitive cycling. From the moment you roll off Qixingtan Beach at dawn, the Pacific Ocean still audible behind you, the road points upward and does not relent for 87 timed kilometers. Through Taroko Gorge — marble walls hundreds of meters high, tunnels carved through the mountain by hand, bridges spanning the Liwu River far below — the road climbs steadily, almost gently, lulling you into believing the gradient is manageable. Then the gorge ends, the valley opens, and the switchbacks begin. The final 10 kilometers to Wuling Pass — where gradients average 17% and spike to 27% at an altitude where the air holds 70% of the oxygen your lungs are accustomed to — are among the hardest paved kilometers in world cycling. When you reach 3,275 meters, you are standing on the highest paved road in Taiwan, above the cloud line, and you rode there from the beach. WorldTour Grand Tour winners have raced this course. The record stands at 3:16:09. Most riders take 4-6 hours. All of them remember it forever.
strengths: Sea level to 3,275m in a single ride — one of the highest continuous paved ascents on Earth, through one of Asia's great natural wonders
Taroko Gorge: marble canyons, hand-carved tunnels, cliff-edge temples, roaring rivers — the most spectacular cycling scenery in Asia
WorldTour-caliber field: Nibali, Yates, Evans, Vos, Pooley have all competed — you race alongside genuine Grand Tour contenders
Extraordinary value: ~$150 entry for a world-class mass-start mountain race with chip timing and elite competition
Complete climate journey: tropical coast to alpine summit, every ecological zone Taiwan possesses, in a single effort
weaknesses: Vulnerability to natural disasters: 2024 canceled mid-race (landslides), traditional route closed after April 2024 earthquake — the course may not be available in any given year
International logistics: 12-20 hour flights for Western riders, jet lag, and a 2-3 hour train to Hualien from Taipei
Altitude at 3,275m is no joke: riders without high-altitude experience may suffer disproportionately in the final 10 km where gradients are steepest
The final 10 km with 27% gradients at altitude is a gear-selection crisis — compact cranks with 34/34 may not be enough
biased_opinion.bottom_line: From the Pacific Ocean to the clouds. Through marble canyons carved over millennia. Up switchbacks that would make an Alpine pass blush. If you climb, this is the pilgrimage.

final_verdict.one_liner: 105 kilometers. Sea level to 3,275 meters. Through Taroko Gorge to the roof of Taiwan. Asia's greatest hill climb.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want one of the world's most spectacular single-day climbing challenges — a ride from the Pacific Ocean to 3,275m through marble canyons and cloud forests. Yes if racing alongside WorldTour professionals in a mass-start field of 40+ nationalities appeals to you. Yes if Taiwan's extraordinary food, culture, and hospitality are as compelling as the climbing. Skip if high altitude (3,275m) intimidates you and you have no acclimatization plan. Skip if the logistical complexity of traveling to eastern Taiwan is a dealbreaker. Skip if the event's vulnerability to weather and earthquake disruption makes you uneasy about booking flights months in advance.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want a high-altitude European road climb: try the Maratona dles Dolomites or the Haute Route Alps. If you want an Asian cycling adventure with less altitude: try the Tour de Okinawa. If you want a pure hill climb at extreme elevation: try the Mt. Evans Hill Climb in Colorado.

logistics.airport: Taiwan Taoyuan International (TPE) — major international hub, 2.5-3 hours to Hualien by train. Taipei Songshan (TSA) — domestic and regional flights, 2-2.5 hours to Hualien by train. Hualien Airport (HUN) — small domestic airport with limited flights from Taipei and Kaohsiung.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Stay in Hualien City the night before — the start at Qixingtan Beach is within 10 km of downtown hotels. Hualien has a wide range of accommodation from budget hostels ($20/night) to quality hotels ($50-120/night). Book during the event week for the best proximity to packet pickup and pre-race logistics. Many international riders arrive in Taipei 1-2 days early for jet lag adjustment and then take the train to Hualien. Tour operator packages (Alpine Cadence, Taiwan KOM Tours) handle all logistics including accommodation, transport, and bike shipping.
logistics.food: Taiwan's food scene is one of the world's great bargains. Hualien's night market offers extraordinary street food — dumplings, noodle soups, grilled seafood, bubble tea — for $2-5 per meal. Restaurant dining is equally affordable and exceptional. Pre-race carb-loading is effortless: rice, noodle, and dumpling options are limitless. Taiwanese food is fuel-efficient: high carbohydrate, moderate protein, and delicious. The night market the evening before the race is a rite of passage for international riders.
logistics.packet_pickup: Event registration and packet pickup in Hualien, typically the day before the race. Organized by the Taiwan Cyclist Federation at a central Hualien venue. Bring passport and registration confirmation.
logistics.parking: Not applicable for most international riders (arrive by train). Local riders can drive to Hualien — parking available near the start at Qixingtan Beach. The start area accommodates the full field with organized staging.

history.founder: Taiwan Cyclist Federation, supported by Taiwan's Tourism Bureau
history.origin_story: The Taiwan KOM Challenge was conceived in 2012 to showcase one of the world's great cycling ascents — the climb from Hualien on Taiwan's Pacific coast through Taroko Gorge to Wuling Pass at 3,275m, the highest paved road in Taiwan. The inaugural edition attracted just 79 riders, but the combination of a world-class course, Taiwan's hospitality, and growing international word-of-mouth drove rapid growth. By 2015, the field had swelled to 412 riders from 32 nationalities. By 2017, when Vincenzo Nibali set the course record at 3:19:54, 600 riders from 32 countries competed. The 2019 edition saw a record 730 riders from 42 countries. The event weathered COVID disruptions and returned strong, with the 2023 edition drawing over 950 riders and producing a new course record (Dyball, 3:16:09). The April 2024 Hualien earthquake severely damaged the Taroko Gorge road, forcing the cancellation of the 2024 edition mid-race and a format change for 2025. The traditional route's return depends on road reconstruction through Taroko National Park.
notable_moments: 2012: Inaugural edition — 79 riders. John Ebsen (Denmark) wins the first Taiwan KOM Challenge in 3:27:25
2013: Eri Yonamine (Japan) wins the first of three consecutive women's titles
2014: John Ebsen returns for his second victory
2015: Field grows to 412 riders from 32 countries. Damien Monier (France) wins
2016: Oscar Pujol (Spain) wins. Emma Pooley (Great Britain) takes women's title in 4:08:40
2017: Vincenzo Nibali wins and sets course record at 3:19:54. Cadel Evans finishes 10th. Prize parity for men and women announced. 600 riders from 32 countries
2018: John Ebsen wins his third title. Lucy Kennedy (Australia) sets women's record at 3:51:19
2019: Record 730 riders from 42 countries. Anthon Charmig wins. Ashleigh Moolman-Pasio wins women's, Marianne Vos second
2022: John Ebsen wins his record fifth Taiwan KOM — a decade of dominance
2023: Benjamin Dyball shatters Nibali's record with 3:16:09. Simon Yates (Jayco-AlUla) competes. Over 950 riders start
2024: Race canceled mid-race after 6 km due to landslides, rockfall, and extreme weather. April 2024 earthquake had severely damaged Taroko Gorge road
2025: Traditional route unavailable. Event runs alternate 203 km Classic road race format. Feng Jun-Kai wins in 5:11:38
history.reputation: Asia's most iconic hill climb. One of the highest and most spectacular paved road ascents on Earth. Where WorldTour champions and amateur climbers share the same start line at sea level and race to the clouds.

course_description.character: The Taiwan KOM Challenge is a 105 km journey from the Pacific Ocean to the roof of Taiwan — a single, continuous ascent from sea level to 3,275 meters that passes through one of the natural wonders of Asia. The first 18.5 km is a neutral rollout along the coast from Qixingtan Beach to the Taroko Gorge entrance, where the official timing begins. From there, the road enters a different world: Taroko Gorge's marble walls rise hundreds of meters on either side, the Liwu River carves through the canyon floor far below, tunnels pierce the rock, and red bridges span impossible gaps. The gradient through the gorge is steady — 5-7% for roughly 50 km — and the scenery is so extraordinary that first-time riders forget they are in a race. Past Tianxiang, the gorge opens into forested mountain valleys. The road continues climbing through temperate forest and alpine meadow. Then comes Dayuling at 2,565m — the psychological halfway point of the high-altitude suffering. The final 10 km from Dayuling to Wuling Pass delivers the coup de grace: switchbacks averaging 17% with pitches to 27%, at an altitude where your lungs can extract only 70% of the oxygen they are used to. The summit at 3,275m is above the cloud line more often than not. You rode there from the beach.

suffering_zones.label: Qixingtan Beach to Taroko Archway — The Coastal Prologue
suffering_zones.desc: 6:00 AM. Dawn light on the Pacific. The full field of 800-1,000 riders rolls out together from the beach square at sea level. The first 18.5 km is a neutral section along the coast and into the mouth of Taroko Gorge. The pace is controlled but the energy is electric — professionals, elite amateurs, and dreamers from 40+ countries sharing the same road. At the Taroko Archway, the flag drops and the clock starts. The race begins.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Flat coastal road, sea level, neutral rollout, Pacific Ocean views

suffering_zones.label: Taroko Gorge — The Marble Cathedral
suffering_zones.desc: The road enters Taroko Gorge and the world transforms. Sheer marble walls rise vertically on both sides — white, grey, and blue stone carved by the Liwu River over millions of years. The road is carved into the cliff face, passing through tunnels hewn by hand in the 1950s. Bright red bridges span gorges hundreds of meters deep. Temples cling to rock faces. The gradient is a steady 2-5% — manageable, deceptive, burning matches you will need later. First-time riders cannot stop looking. The gorge is so spectacular that the climbing almost feels secondary. It is not. Every meter of elevation gained here is a meter you will not need to gain later, and the oxygen debt is accumulating.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Narrow gorge road, 2-5% gradient, tunnels, bridges, marble cliffs, 0-500m elevation
suffering_zones.named_section: Taroko Gorge — Swallow Grotto to Tianxiang

suffering_zones.label: Tianxiang to Dayuling — The Long Grind
suffering_zones.desc: Past the gorge settlement of Tianxiang, the valley opens. The marble walls recede. The road begins to climb with more intent — 5-7% average gradients through forested valleys, across open mountain meadows, past occasional mountain settlements. This is the grinding heart of the race: 40+ km of sustained climbing at an effort level that sorts the field decisively. The elevation ticks upward relentlessly — 1,000m, 1,500m, 2,000m, 2,500m. The air thins. The temperature drops. By Dayuling at 2,565m, you have been climbing for 2-4 hours and the summit is still 700 vertical meters and 10 horizontal kilometers away. The hardest part has not yet begun.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Sustained 5-7% gradient, forested valleys, open meadows, 500-2,565m elevation
suffering_zones.named_section: Central Cross-Island Highway — Tianxiang to Dayuling

suffering_zones.label: Dayuling to Wuling Pass — The Final Reckoning
suffering_zones.desc: Dayuling at 2,565m is where the Taiwan KOM reveals its true nature. The final 10 km to Wuling Pass delivers switchbacks with an average gradient of 17% and maximum pitches of 27%. At this altitude, your power output has dropped 15-20% from sea level values. Your heart rate is maxed. Your legs — which have been climbing for hours — are asked to produce their highest sustained power of the day on the steepest gradients. The air is cold, possibly near freezing. Fog may reduce visibility to meters. The switchbacks are relentless. Each hairpin reveals another pitch above. The cloud line may be below you. When the road finally levels at 3,275m — Wuling Pass, the highest paved road in Taiwan — you have completed one of the great climbing feats in cycling. You started at the Pacific Ocean. You are standing above the clouds.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Savage switchbacks, 17% average, 27% maximum gradient, 2,565-3,275m elevation, thin air, cold, fog

course_description.signature_challenge: The final 10 km from Dayuling to Wuling Pass. The combination of extreme gradient (17% average, 27% max) and genuine high altitude (2,565-3,275m) on legs that have already climbed for 3-5 hours creates a physiological crisis that no amount of sea-level training fully prepares you for. The oxygen deficit compounds the muscular fatigue. The cold compounds both. This is where the Taiwan KOM separates itself from every other hill climb on Earth — it does not merely climb high, it climbs brutally to get there.

guide_variables.race_terrain: Paved mountain road through Taroko Gorge to Wuling Pass summit at 3,275m
guide_variables.race_weather: Tropical start at sea level (20-25C), alpine summit finish (0-5C). Temperature drops ~6C per 1,000m of ascent.
race_challenges: 3,275m of continuous climbing from sea level — one of the highest single ascents in world cycling
Final 10 km averages 17% gradient with 27% maximum pitches at 2,500-3,275m altitude
Oxygen at summit is ~70% of sea level — significant power loss for non-acclimatized riders
25C temperature swing from tropical coast to near-freezing alpine summit
Taroko Gorge tunnels: some unlit, narrow, in a mass-start field
Event vulnerability to typhoons, earthquakes, and landslides in eastern Taiwan

non_negotiables.requirement: High-altitude climbing simulation — at least two training sessions at or above 2,500m, or altitude tent acclimatization protocol
non_negotiables.why: The summit at 3,275m is genuine high altitude. Power output drops 15-20% compared to sea level. Riders without altitude experience will hit a wall in the final 10 km where the steepest gradients coincide with the thinnest air. If you cannot access altitude for training, consider arriving in Taiwan 3-5 days early and doing acclimatization rides on the Wuling approach (if accessible) or other high-altitude roads. Altitude tent protocols can help but are no substitute for time at elevation.

non_negotiables.requirement: Sustained climbing endurance — at least two training rides with 3+ hours of continuous climbing
non_negotiables.why: The timed section is 87 km of unbroken climbing. Even at elite pace, this is 3+ hours of sustained uphill effort. For most riders, it is 4-6 hours. Your training must include rides where you climb continuously for 3+ hours without significant descents or flat recovery. If you live in flat terrain, simulate with trainer sessions: 3-4 hours at climbing intensity with no recovery breaks.

non_negotiables.requirement: Gearing for 27% gradients at altitude — test your lowest gear under load
non_negotiables.why: The final 10 km reaches 27% gradient at 3,000m altitude. Many riders' standard compact setups (50/34 x 11-32) are not low enough. Consider a 34/34 or lower combination. Test this gearing on the steepest local climb you can find, at the end of a long ride when your legs are spent. If you are grinding at 40 rpm on a 15% local climb after 4 hours, you need lower gearing for what awaits above Dayuling.

non_negotiables.requirement: Summit clothing strategy — layers accessible without stopping
non_negotiables.why: You will start in summer kit at sea level in 22C humidity. You will finish in near-freezing conditions at 3,275m, possibly in fog and wind. You need a thermal layer, arm warmers, and wind vest that you can access from jersey pockets without stopping. The descent from the summit is long and cold — hypothermia is a real risk if you summit in a sweat-soaked jersey with no insulation. Pack layers in your pockets before the start.
